name: Sound
subject: physics
summary: Sound is a vibration travelling through a medium. Pitch, loudness, frequency, amplitude — and why sound can't travel in vacuum.

crushed.title: Sound = vibration
crushed.text: Object vibrates → particles of medium bump into neighbours → wave travels. Needs solid, liquid, or gas.

crushed.title: No medium = no sound
crushed.text: Space is silent — no air for vibrations to travel. Bell jar experiment proves this.

crushed.title: Pitch = frequency
crushed.text: High frequency → high pitch (flute). Low frequency → low pitch (drum). Measured in hertz (Hz).

crushed.title: Loudness = amplitude
crushed.text: Bigger vibration → louder sound. Measured in decibels (dB). Above 80 dB damages hearing.

crushed.title: Human hearing: 20 Hz – 20,000 Hz
crushed.text: Below 20 Hz = infrasonic (elephants). Above 20,000 Hz = ultrasonic (bats, ultrasound scans).

items.label: Wavelength
items.detail: Distance between consecutive compressions or rarefactions

items.label: Speed
items.detail: Fastest in solids > liquids > gases. ~340 m/s in air at 20°C

items.label: Echo
items.detail: Reflection of sound. Heard when distance > 17 m (0.1 s gap)

items.label: Ultrasonic (> 20,000 Hz)
items.detail: Bats navigate, ultrasound medical scans, cleaning jewellery

flows.title: How Sound Travels Through Air
flows.description: A drum hit creates compressions and rarefactions that reach your ear.

steps.label: Drum hit
steps.description: Strike causes drum membrane to vibrate back and forth.

steps.label: Compression
steps.description: Vibration pushes nearby air particles closer — high pressure zone.

steps.label: Rarefaction
steps.description: Membrane moves back — particles spread apart — low pressure zone.

steps.label: Wave travels
steps.description: Alternating compressions & rarefactions travel outward as longitudinal wave.

steps.label: Ear
steps.description: Wave hits eardrum → vibrations to brain via nerves.

flows.title: Bell Jar Experiment — Sound Needs Medium
flows.description: Electric bell in sealed jar — sound fades as air is pumped out.

steps.label: Setup
steps.description: Ringing electric bell inside airtight glass jar.

steps.label: Air present
steps.description: Vibrations travel through air to outside.

steps.label: Pump air out
steps.description: Vacuum pump removes air from jar gradually.

steps.label: Bell still rings
steps.description: Bell vibrates but no medium to carry sound waves.

keyTerms.definition: Wave where particles vibrate parallel to direction of travel
keyTerms.analogy: A slinky pushed end-to-end — compressions travel along it

keyTerms.definition: Number of vibrations per second, measured in hertz (Hz)
keyTerms.analogy: How fast a guitar string wiggles back and forth

keyTerms.definition: Maximum displacement from rest position — determines loudness
keyTerms.analogy: How far you swing the door — bigger swing, louder bang

keyTerms.definition: Unit of sound loudness. Whisper ~30 dB, jet engine ~140 dB

demos.title: Sound — Vibration to Wave
demos.description: A vibrating bell creates compressions that travel through air as sound.

steps.label: Classroom view
steps.description: Wide shot of the lab — a bell mounted on the wall.

steps.label: Bell vibrates
steps.description: Zoom in: hammer strikes the bell — metal vibrates rapidly.

steps.label: Sound waves
steps.description: Vibrations create compressions in air — waves travel to your ear.

chapterSummary.overview: Sound is a form of energy produced by vibrating objects and travels as longitudinal waves through a medium. This chapter covers production and propagation of sound, characteristics (amplitude, frequency, pitch, loudness), speed in different media, reflection (echo), and the human ear.
keyPoints: Sound needs a medium — cannot travel through vacuum; fastest in solids, slowest in gases.
Vibration → compression and rarefaction → sound wave travels to the ear.
Amplitude → loudness; frequency → pitch; higher frequency = higher pitch.
Human hearing range: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz; ultrasound above 20,000 Hz.
Echo: reflected sound heard after original; used in SONAR and medical imaging.
chapterSummary.examFocus: Explain how sound is produced and travels. Differentiate pitch vs loudness. Calculate echo distance using speed of sound.
